utterances: Einstein was very concerned that the public should understand his ideas and, you know, that came out in a lot of interviews and a lot of humanitarian things he did later on.
But he was also, you know, he was very serious about it as a writer as well.
And then just to round out the list, at number nine, Richard Dawkins' ""Selfish Gene,"" getting us well into the 20th century.
And number 10, ""One Two Three...Infinity"" by George Gamow.
One of the great writers, one of the great science writers.
Gamow, a great writer and just sort of like - just a great, warm human being.
I mean that - you know, basically, the list gets sort of steadily more readable as you move down, with the exception of Darwin who actually is a surprisingly approachable writer.
But, yeah, Gamow is just - you don't - you forget that a physicist can be such a warm and funny human being.
Well, speaking of warm and funny - well, maybe not so warm.
I'm thinking of Feynman, who also wrote a lot (unintelligible) 20th century.
Right.
And the Feynman physics lectures, which are not exactly warm and fuzzy if you try to read them.
(Soundbite of laughter)
You make the mistake of thinking that because people love them, they're approachable.
I mean they're tough going.
But, you know - I mean if you are interested in physics that is the place to start.

And if you're not serious about the physics, his two biographies, which are, you know, wonderfully snarky, are a great place to sort of get into the mind of a physicist without wrestling with the actual science.
Right.
1-800-989-8255 is the number.
Phil Manning, give me your picks for the top science books of this year.
I picked out really three books, or three sorts of books.
They tend to fall into a couple of categories.
One is a book that makes a point on some topic that's very relevant today.
And there I picked out Tim Flannery's book, ""The Weather Markers"" is the title of it.
And it's about global warming, and it's almost impossible to read that book and not come away believing that global warming has occurred, is occurring, is going to continue to occur.
That's like Al Gore's book.
Al Gore's book would be the same kind of read.
Yeah, and of course Al Gore published a book on the subject, what, a decade ago anyway, that I also read.

But this book - Tim Flannery is an Australian scientist, and he's just really good in marshalling the facts and putting things together so that you can see it very clearly, and it makes a very good scientific case for global warming.

Then there's a couple of books that I was really interested in that - one of which you mentioned earlier, ""The Language of God"" by Francis Collins, because it reminded me of a book that came out a little bit earlier.
It came out in October of 2005.
It's called ""Our Inner Ape"" by a man named Frans de Waal, who is a primatologist at Emory University.
Right.
And these two guys, both of them, are tending to approach how we developed, humans developed, this moral code, so to speak.
In ""The Language of God"" Francis Collins is very clear.
He says, look, we have this moral code and we have it because God gave it to us.
Good afternoon, Neal.

Frans de Waal, on the other hand, who studied chimpanzees for decades and decades and decades and has seen many, many, many examples of chimpanzee altruism, he says basically - and almost in diametric opposed to what Collins says - he says, look our culture, our morality did not come from the pins of philosophy, it did not come from a religion, it did come from culture, it came from the apes.
And he believes it, and he makes a very, very strong case for that.

The last book that I - another book that I really, really liked this year was a book called ""The Ghost Map"" by Steven Johnson, which is a book that describes how one man, Dr. John Snow, tracked down the source of cholera transmission in London.
And what's interesting about the book - and the fact that if he hadn't tracked it down, we probably wouldn't have London because we would still have these cholera epidemics.
But more importantly, it was one man that did it.
Can you imagine in today's age of science and the collaborative nature of almost everything in science today...
No.
...one person accomplishing something of this magnitude.
It's almost impossible to imagine.
If we had a cholera epidemic or something like that, equivalent of a cholera epidemic, today we'd have how many - we'd have 500 people on it within days, you know.
He did it all by himself.
Well, look how many people are working on that white powder they were looking for.
That's right.
You know.
There were hundreds of people working on it.
But it was remarkable what this one man did essentially acting on his own.
And Steven Johnson makes a wonderful story out of this, a beautiful narrative.
Corey, what do you suggest?
Give me a couple before we go to break.
What do you have on your list?
For this year…
Yeah.
…rather for the breakout of all time?
Yeah.
Actually, I agree with some of those choices.
You mentioned Michael Pollan's book, which is certainly one of the most enjoyable and readable books.
And I think also it makes an important point.
There are a lot of interesting juxtapositions.
And you mentioned the Richard Dawkins' book and the Francis Collins books as sort of taking two sides on the relationship between science and God.

And a third book, which I think certainly belongs in that group, Daniel Dennett's book, "Breaking the Spell," which is an interesting tangled, sometimes infuriating attempt to really just putting science absolutely like a steamroller in the way of religion and just saying we need to stop and take a look, you know, take a look at what religion truly is.
Whatever you think of his thesis, it's a very provocative book.
And on climate, I think Elizabeth Kolbert's "Field Notes from a Catastrophe" certainly rings up there with Tim Flannery's book.
I actually reviewed that book as well, so.
Yeah.
And, you know, I thought it was quite good.
I'm going to get to Heather when we come back from a break.
I want to give her plenty of time.
We have just about a minute to go.
But I want to ask you, Corey, do you think any book out today is going to make your top 25 list 25 years from now?
Twenty-five years from now.
Twenty-five years from now.

You know, I think it's possible that we're going to look back on this era - I actually think the sort of - the science and God debate going on right now is something that we're going to see as something very characteristic of this era.

And I think, you know, 25 years from now when people look back, they are going to look at this - at, you know, the Dawkins book and the Dennett book and the Collins book, that these were things that really captured a time of intellectual ferment.
And I think they could - I think those are the ones probably that would still - that would stay on the list.
(Unintelligible) the Scopes trial did 80-so years ago.
Right, and when you read the Scopes trial, you read it not just because, you know - I mean "Inherit the Wind"...
Right.
...you know, as a play or as a movie, it looks very dated now and yet it's still fascinating as a snapshot of kind of where the intellectual argument was at the time.
And I think people will look at these science and God books the same way, not so much as that that's the state of the art of thinking as this is a very interesting stage in a debate.
Was there a topic that wasn't covered this year that you think might have been left out?

To me the most surprising thing - I mean given where we are as a country, where things are politically, is that there really - I mean there were some very notable climate books, but there really wasn't anybody who wrote a book about energy, about sort of where we are with fossil fuels or about how we break out of this cycle.
Nobody really captured it in a way that, say, Elizabeth Kolbert with climate.
And I feel like that's a story waiting to be told.
I mean that's - or is an argument waiting to be made in a really powerful way.
We've had a lot of academic books, which we've reviewed, on here, but none - no popular - you're right.
Right.
A ton of those books, but nothing...
Yes, a ton.
Right, there's an enormous amount of research going on, an enormous amount of discussion going on, but nothing that I think is really tapping into hitting the public nerve.
Well, there's a new administration.
We'll see what happens.
